metadata.title: “Only visible for three days”: Mining microblogs to understand reasons for using the Time Limit setting on WeChat Moments

abstract: Social media platforms are increasingly implementing ephemerality settings to meet users' need to manage past posts. However, few studies discuss factors influencing the use of ephemerality-related designs on social media. Using a text mining approach, we explore users' main reasons for choosing the Time Limit setting on WeChat Moments. We apply the latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) method to analyze a dataset of 26,059 Weibo microblogs and comments relating to the use of the three-day limit on WeChat Moments. Our results indicate that evolving self-presentation, life changes, changes to the social network, the burden of impression management, the desire to retain a sense of mystery, privacy protection, mood state, and the use of substitute platforms are potential factors. We further indicate that changes in life and social networks may lead to evolving selfpresentation and increase the burden of impression management. Our findings may provide a foundation for further scholarly exploration of ephemerality on social media and inspire practitioners to design more granular ephemerality settings.

paragraphs: Most social media platforms (e.g., Facebook) archive users' digital traces by default (Treem & Leonardi, 2013), allowing users to record meaningful events, engage in long-term self-presentation, and enhance their social capital (Zhao et al., 2013). However, data permanence on social media may add uncertainty to future interactions, as some past posts (e.g., childish or over-emotional content) may be inconsistent with users' current and future self-presentation (Huang et al., 2020;Schoenebeck et al., 2016). Research has revealed some users are concerned about their past posts on social media (Xu et al., 2016;Zhao et al., 2013) and expect the Internet to automatically delete or hide their past information (Novotny & Spiekermann, 2014). To deal with challenges caused by data permanence, social media platforms (e.g., Snapchat, Instagram Stories, and WeChat Moments) are increasingly implementing ephemerality settings that make users' messages and posts inaccessible after a specific period (Bayer et al., 2016;Huang et al., 2020). Among them, Snapchat is a well-known ephemeral social media platform, which destroys users' messages in a short time (Xu et al., 2016).

Similarly, WeChat Moments (abbreviated as "Moments"), part of the most popular social media platform in China, has launched the Time Limit setting that allows users to choose an expiry time (three days, one month, or six months) for their posts, after which content is only viewable by posters themselves. The three-day limit (which hides posted content after three days) is particularly popular (Huang et al., 2020;Li et al., 2018). More than 200 million Moments users chose this option in January 2021 (Tencent, 2021). Understanding users' opinions on new features is a basic requirement for identifying their needs, and in turn provides new insights into design settings to enhance user experience (Li et al., 2018). Hence, some scholars have investigated the Time Limit setting on Moments through interviews (Huang et al., 2020;Zhang, Wang, et al., 2021;Zheng & Zhao, 2020) and surveys (Ma et al., 2021;Zhang, Tan, et al., 2021). However, we still know little about reasons for using the Time Limit setting; there is a need for more empirical studies to reveal users' perceptions of this new feature.

Individuals generate millions of messages per day on microblog platforms (e.g., Twitter), and this huge volume of user-generated content (UGC) contains information on individuals' thoughts and behaviors, which can be analyzed and turned into meaningful knowledge (Bian et al., 2016;Wang et al., 2020). Particularly, topic modeling is a popular method of extracting hidden themes or topics based on large volumes of documents (Blei, 2012), and it has been widely used to study issues across research areas (e.g., Bian et al., 2016;Ibrahim & Wang, 2019). Moreover, the three-day limit on Moments is a hot topic on a popular Chinese microblogging platform, Sina Weibo (abbreviated as "Weibo"), where individuals discuss users' reasons for choosing this setting. Accordingly, we utilize the topic modeling method to explore reasons for using the Time Limit setting on Moments, based on relevant microblogs.

Our work contributes to the literature in the following ways. First, we enrich the literature on ephemerality on social media by identifying potential antecedents of using ephemerality-related designs; the limited number of studies focused mainly on the consequences of ephemerality (Bayer et al., 2016;Cavalcanti et al., 2017). Whereas most relevant research explored ephemerality based on Snapchat (McRoberts et al., 2017) or Instagram Stories (Bainotti et al., 2020), we select the popular Time Limit setting on Moments as an example, thereby extending the literature. Second, we deepen understanding of reasons for using the Time Limit setting through a text mining approach. Although some studies have discussed the Time Limit setting using interviews or surveys (e.g., Huang et al., 2020;Ma et al., 2021;Zhang, Wang, et al., 2021, b), this is the first study to analyze this new feature using big data. Finally, we believe our findings may provide useful guidelines for practitioners designing ephemeral settings and in turn improve users' experiences on social media.

paragraphs: Social media platforms (e.g., Snapchat, Instagram Stories) that enable users to share ephemeral content are becoming increasingly popular, and research on the content ephemerality has been discussed across different fields (Alhabash & Ma, 2017;Billings et al., 2015). However, existing research on the ephemerality of social media mainly focused on the outcomes of using ephemeral social media (especially on Snapchat), but only limited studies explored relevant antecedents. Next, we attempt to summary relevant findings and compare our work with prior similar studies.

sections.paper_id: Zhang et al. - 2022 - “Only visible for three days” Mining microblogs to understand reasons for using the Time Limit sett
sections.header_raw: The outcomes of using ephemeral social media
sections.header_norm: the outcomes of using ephemeral social media
paragraphs: Most previous studies explored how ephemeral content impacted user experiences and behaviors by focusing mostly on Snapchat (Bayer et al., 2016;Choi et al., 2020;Xu et al., 2016). In particular, research has indicated that sharing ephemeral content on Snapchat can elevate perceived enjoyment (Bayer et al., 2016;Morlok et al., 2017), reduce self-presentation concerns (Bayer et al., 2016;Choi et al., 2020), decrease the effort required for impression management (Choi et al., 2020), and increase information disclosure (Ma et al., 2021;Morlok et al., 2018). For example, Xu et al. (2016) explored the ephemerality affordance on Snapchat and found that users find value in ephemeral communication, which can reduce their self-consciousness, prevent the accumulation of embarrassing content, and decrease their worries about unintended audiences. Ephemerality can also help users to express their authentic selves (Choi et al., 2020) and support their evolving self-identity (Huang et al., 2020).

Nonetheless, posting ephemeral content may also have negative consequences, involving receiving less social support (Bayer et al., 2016), undermining social relationships (Huang et al., 2020;Li et al., 2018), and experiencing forms of loss (Cavalcanti et al., 2017). For instance, Li et al. (2018) claimed that using the Time Limit setting on Moments could undermine social relationships and induce emotional disturbance for both posters and audiences. That is, posters may feel embarrassed and guilty about using this feature, and audiences may feel disappointed and rejected. Similarly, Huang et al. (2020) found that individuals may form negative impressions of new friends who use an overly restrictive Time Limit setting (i.e., the three-day option) on Moments, such as by considering them defensive, isolated, unfriendly, and aloof. Cavalcanti et al. (2017) investigated the relationship between ephemerality and loss on social media and found that users experience three types of loss, namely media loss (e.g., an unsaved photo), meaning loss (e.g., the inability to revisit a snap to reminisce about a particular moment), and context loss (e.g., a lack of message history). In particular, ephemerality prevents users from displaying meaningful past posts (e.g., travel photos) in the long term and making new friends quickly (Huang et al., 2020).

sections.header_raw: The antecedents of using ephemeral social media
sections.header_norm: the antecedents of using ephemeral social media
paragraphs: There were a few studies discussed the antecedents of using ephemeral social media, and we summarized relevant literature in the following Table 1. We can see most of prior studies mainly focused on one of the most popular ephemeral social media-Snapchat, and investigated the reasons (Piwek & Joinson, 2016;Roesner et al., 2014), motivations (Billings et al., 2017;Spinda & Puckette, 2018) and gratifications (Phua et al., 2017;Waddell, 2016) for using this platform. Particularly, these studies indicated that individuals utilized Snapchat more for fun, convenience, passing time, self-expression, and relationship maintenance rather than privacy concerns (Alhabash & Ma, 2017;Choi & Sung, 2018;Kircaburun et al., 2020;Punyanunt-Carter et al., 2017). Likewise,Cheung (2019) explored users' motivations for engaging in ephemeral content on general social media and found fear of missing out, trust, immediacy, and social pressure impacted gratification, which in turn enhanced engagement with ephemeral content. Moreover, scholars also investigated what other factors drove Snapchat use including technology acceptance (Coa & Setiawan, 2017;Lemay et al., 2017), privacy (Lemay et al., 2017;Morlok et al., 2017), social presence (Jeong & Lee, 2017;;Kırçova et al., 2020), and relationship (Kahlow et al., 2020;Makki et al., 2018).

To the best of our knowledge, there were only two study explored why users applied the Time Limit setting on Moments through grounded theory. Zheng and Zhao (2020) identified 16 factors (e.g., social interaction needs, self-expressing, or usefulness) involving in social, psychological, and technical aspects through interviews with 16 users. Zhang, Wang, et al. (2021) proposed a conceptual framework along with six aspect factors regarding adopt the Time Limit setting through grounded theory (e.g., past information factors, motivations, the benefits and costs of using the setting). Moreover, three other relevant studies also mentioned reasons for using the Time Limit setting when they focused on other research topics. Li et al. (2018) argued that the presence of supervisors in social networks and the burden of audience segment lead to Time Limit setting use. Huang et al. (2020) claimed changes in self-presentation goals and social network composition over time could encourage users to choose the Time Limit setting. Zhang, Tan, et al. (2021) investigated personal and social characteristics of Time Limit users and found users were associated with higher levels of posting frequency and privacy setting use as well as smaller audience size compared with non-users.

Based on the above, research on ephemerality in social media is still in its infancy. Most existing studies mainly focus on Snapchat, especially its "Chat" feature (messages self-destruct up to 10 s) rather than "Stories" (posts disappear in 24 h). As such, some scholars consider Snapchat is more like an ephemeral instant messaging service (Makki et al., 2018;Piwek & Joinson, 2016). However, less attentions have been paid to other platforms with ephemeral affordances (e.g., Instagram Stories, WeChat Moments). Since different ephemerality-related designs would offer different temporal experiences for users (Ma et al., 2021), there is need to explore users' attitudes and behaviors based on other ephemeral social media (Bainotti et al., 2020).

Since different ephemerality-related designs would offer different temporal experiences for users (Ma et al., 2021), there is need to explore users' attitudes and behaviors based on other ephemeral social media (Bainotti et al., 2020).
sections.text_with_footnotes:
There were a few studies discussed the antecedents of using ephemeral social media, and we summarized relevant literature in the following Table 1. We can see most of prior studies mainly focused on one of the most popular ephemeral social media-Snapchat, and investigated the reasons (Piwek & Joinson, 2016;Roesner et al., 2014), motivations (Billings et al., 2017;Spinda & Puckette, 2018) and gratifications (Phua et al., 2017;Waddell, 2016) for using this platform. Particularly, these studies indicated that individuals utilized Snapchat more for fun, convenience, passing time, self-expression, and relationship maintenance rather than privacy concerns (Alhabash & Ma, 2017;Choi & Sung, 2018;Kircaburun et al., 2020;Punyanunt-Carter et al., 2017). Likewise,Cheung (2019) explored users' motivations for engaging in ephemeral content on general social media and found fear of missing out, trust, immediacy, and social pressure impacted gratification, which in turn enhanced engagement with ephemeral content. Moreover, scholars also investigated what other factors drove Snapchat use including technology acceptance (Coa & Setiawan, 2017;Lemay et al., 2017), privacy (Lemay et al., 2017;Morlok et al., 2017), social presence (Jeong & Lee, 2017;;Kırçova et al., 2020), and relationship (Kahlow et al., 2020;Makki et al., 2018).

To the best of our knowledge, there were only two study explored why users applied the Time Limit setting on Moments through grounded theory. Zheng and Zhao (2020) identified 16 factors (e.g., social interaction needs, self-expressing, or usefulness) involving in social, psychological, and technical aspects through interviews with 16 users. Zhang, Wang, et al. (2021) proposed a conceptual framework along with six aspect factors regarding adopt the Time Limit setting through grounded theory (e.g., past information factors, motivations, the benefits and costs of using the setting). Moreover, three other relevant studies also mentioned reasons for using the Time Limit setting when they focused on other research topics. Li et al. (2018) argued that the presence of supervisors in social networks and the burden of audience segment lead to Time Limit setting use. Huang et al. (2020) claimed changes in self-presentation goals and social network composition over time could encourage users to choose the Time Limit setting. Zhang, Tan, et al. (2021) investigated personal and social characteristics of Time Limit users and found users were associated with higher levels of posting frequency and privacy setting use as well as smaller audience size compared with non-users.

Based on the above, research on ephemerality in social media is still in its infancy. Most existing studies mainly focus on Snapchat, especially its "Chat" feature (messages self-destruct up to 10 s) rather than "Stories" (posts disappear in 24 h). As such, some scholars consider Snapchat is more like an ephemeral instant messaging service (Makki et al., 2018;Piwek & Joinson, 2016). However, less attentions have been paid to other platforms with ephemeral affordances (e.g., Instagram Stories, WeChat Moments). Since different ephemerality-related designs would offer different temporal experiences for users (Ma et al., 2021), there is need to explore users' attitudes and behaviors based on other ephemeral social media (Bainotti et al., 2020).

sections.header_raw: Time Limit setting on WeChat Moments
sections.header_norm: time limit setting on wechat moments
paragraphs: WeChat Moments (part of WeChat) gives users a profile page and allows them to post content and interact. This platform is similar to Facebook, but only a user's WeChat friends can view their posts. Moments launched the Time Limit setting in 2017, which allows users to set an expiration date for their posts. The current options are three days, one month, six months, and no limit (shown in Appendix A; "all" means no limit). After the selected time frame (e.g., three days), posts are hidden from all contacts and can only be viewed by users themselves. When a user applies the Time Limit setting, their WeChat friends see the notice "Only [Time Limit] of Moments are viewable" on the profile (shown in Appendix B). Among them, the three-day limit has received considerable attention and aroused public controversy. As such, we take the three-day limit as an example to understand usage of the Time Limit setting on Moments.

sections.paper_id: Zhang et al. - 2022 - “Only visible for three days” Mining microblogs to understand reasons for using the Time Limit sett
paragraphs: Sina Weibo, a Twitter-like platform, is one of the most popular social media in China, with approximately 530 million monthly active users in May 2021 (Sina Finance, 2021). It has been used as a main data source in various studies, such as on information diffusion (Wang et al., 2020) and topic discovery (Fan et al., 2015). Similar to Twitter, Weibo allows users to share their daily lives, opinions, and experiences as well as to interact with others (Wang et al., 2020). Hence, we chose Weibo as the data source to explore users' perceptions of the three-day limit setting. The Time Limit setting on Moments, launched in March 2017, has attracted attention on Weibo; people often use the keyword "three-day visibility (三天可见)" when they discuss this feature. By searching for the keyword "three-day visibility" on Weibo, we found 112 relevant topics published between March 2017 (the launch date of "three-day" option) and November 2020, with more than 1.8 billion views and discussions (including microblogs and comments; an example is shown in Appendix C) .

To improve the data accuracy, we collected microblogs and comments on relevant discussion topics. After excluding topics unrelated to our research questions (e.g., a reality show named Only 3 days), topics remained. The 10 most discussed topics and the number of discussions of those topics are shown in Fig. 2. Next, we used Gooseeker (a Web crawler) to collect all microblogs and comments from the relevant topics. The raw database contained 41,286 posts. We removed non-original microblogs that were reposts (distinguishable by//@). Next, we removed microblogs that were not related to the three-day visibility setting. Finally, we obtained 26,059 valid posts.

paragraphs: To obtain small and meaningful units, we used Jieba (a widely used segmentation tool in Python) to segment microblog texts. As a large proportion of Chinese words are made up of two or more characters, we removed words of only one character. We found a large number of words that appeared frequently but were irrelevant to our analysis (such as the word "setting"). To delete these words, we built a custom dictionary. Also, we removed "stop words" from each text, which are important from a grammatical perspective but do not contain independent meaning. We did this based on the stopped words library published by the Harbin Institute of Technology and the Machine Intelligence Laboratory of Sichuan University.

paragraphs: A topic modeling approach, part of machine learning and natural language processing, is used to extract merged topics based on large volumes of textual data (Gerrish & Blei, 2011). The basic premise is that a document is a random mixture of topics and a topic is a combination of words satisfying a certain probability distribution (Blei, 2012). The popular topic modelling methods include Latent Semantic Indexing (LSI) (Deerwester et al., 1990), Probabilistic Latent Semantic Analysis (PLSA) (Hofmann, 1999), and Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) (Blei et al., 2003). Based on previous research practice, LDA had better performance in big data mining than the above two methods, as it was a three-level Hierarchical Bayesian model with a clearer internal structure as well as its word bag model made it more easily to build model (Pathan and Prakash, 2021). In particular, LDA considers any document as created from several latent topics, where each topic is presented with a mixture of words. The LDA's data-generation process (shown in Fig. 3) has the following four main steps:

1. Choose a document m randomly. 2. Sample a topic proportion θ m from Dirichlet distribution Dir (α). 3. Select a topic z m,n randomly from the proportion θ m . 4. Pick a word w m,n randomly from the multinomial distribution φ k of the previously chosen topic z m,n .

paragraphs: Determining the optimal number of topics has always been a challenge in the LDA approach (Kim et al., 2016), although most research has set a pre-defined number of topics, such as 10 or 20, based on human judgment (Fischer-Preßler et al., 2019;Kim et al., 2016). Using too many topics may add difficulty to making interpretations, and using too few may be insufficient to accurately identify dimensions (Nikolenko et al., 2017). Thus, we used the perplexity-based approach (Zhao et al., 2015) to identify the optimal number of topics in our dataset. This approach been applied in previous studies (e.g., Son et al., 2020;Vu et al., 2019). The perplexity is related to the inverse of the geometric mean per-word likelihood (Zhao et al., 2015). It is an iterative method in which models with different numbers of topics are compared based on the corresponding perplexity score. The lower the perplexity score, the better the model performance. Specifically, perplexity is defined as follows: By applying the perplexity measure, we obtained the optimal number of topics in our dataset, namely 10, as shown in Fig. 4. Fig. 5. Topic distribution.

Zhang et al. - 2022 - “Only visible for three days” Mining microblogs to understand reasons for using the Time Limit sett
paragraphs: As discussed above, the LDA approach identified 10 topics. The 10 most prevalent words in each topic, their relative weights and the number of corresponding documents are shown in Table 3, and the distribution of topics and their word associations are presented in Fig. 5.

The procedure of qualitative analysis is based on prior research on computer-assisted content analysis (Hagen, 2018;Nelson, 2020). Specifically, two trained coders with domain knowledge about ephemerality research in social media read through the top 10 most probable words (shown in Table 3) and the top 20 most representative documents (10 documents with the highest probability, and other 10 documents randomly selected from the remaining documents) (examples shown in Appendix D) for each topic. They independently assigned labels to topics. After that, the two coders discussed the coding results and generated a reconciled set of labels. Then, as suggested by prior research (Jung & Suh, 2019;Wu & Chang, 2020), two other researchers assigned the labels to 200 randomly sampled documents to calculate the inter-rater reliability. As shown in Table 2, the degrees of agreement between LDA and researcher A, between LDA and researcher B, as well as between researcher A and researcher B are 0.556, 0.481, and 0.640, respectively, suggesting moderate agreement (Cohen, 1960). Most of the Kappa coefficients of each topic are moderate or above. The low agreement of some labels may be because one document may mention two topics at once while we assign only one topic for each document. Accordingly, our LDA results are reliable to some extent (Cohen, 1960;Jung & Suh, 2019).

As shown in Table 3, the 10 topics were past self, desire to retain mystery, life changes, burden of audience segmentation, use of substitute platforms, changes to the social network, mood state, past photos, burden of old content deletion, and privacy protection. We provide details of the coding procedure for one of the topics (i.e., Topic 4) to illustrate the validity of qualitative results. Topic 4 was labeled "burden of audience segmentation" after analysis of corresponding word distribution and representative posts. This topic contained highly relevant words, such as "segmentation" and "block." Microblogs associated with this topic mainly focused on how audience segmentation is cumbersome and time-consuming, especially when individuals graduate and begin work. Users were also worried that their blocking behavior may be revealed, resulting in embarrassment or conflict. As such, users employed the Time Limit setting instead of audience segmentation. The topic included relevant phrases such as "As I have too many friends, I don't want to segment my audiences before posting, so I employ the three-day limit setting" or "I don't want those who are tagged out (i.e., unable to see certain posts) to find out they are blocked by me."

As past self and past photos are related to inconsistencies between old and new self-presentation, we organized them into a single category regarding evolving self-presentation, based on prior studies (Huang et al., 2020;Luria & Foulds, 2021). In addition, burden of audience segmentation and burden of old content deletion were categorized as burden of impression management. We next discuss how the final eight dimensions impact the use of the Time Limit setting on Moments.

paragraphs: As suggested by Hagen (2018), a good validation is to compare the LDA-generated topics with human-generated themes. Accordingly, we compared our results with findings from two prior relevant research on exploring influencing factors of using the Time Limit setting on Moments through grounded theory (GT) (Zheng & Zhao, 2020;Zhang, Wang, et al., 2021). While machine learning could not extract the high-level conceptual patterns identified by humans based on grounded theory (Baumer et al., 2017), we compared our topics with themes identified from the open coding (Glaser & Strauss, 1967). As shown in Table 4, although there is no exactly one-to-one correspondence between topics and themes, most topics resonate with at least one or more themes. Specifically, most of our topics are included in the study of Zheng and Zhao (2020), excepting one topic (i.e., past self). Likewise, all of our topics could be founded in the study of Zhang, Wang, et al. (2021). Thus, our LDA-generated topics could be validated. These results are consistent with prior research which indicated that topic modelling results can align with results from human-only interpretivist analyses (Baumer et al., 2017;Hagen, 2018). However, due to the advantage of grounded theory with more contextual information (Baumer et al., 2017), several influencing factors of using the Time Limit setting are not identified by computer-assisted content analysis, such as individual characteristics (e. g., introversion), herd mentality, to avoid disturbances or to be concentrated on work, feel pressure, as well as the cost of using the Time Limit setting (e.g., being considered as aloof or not candid).

sections.paper_id: Zhang et al. - 2022 - “Only visible for three days” Mining microblogs to understand reasons for using the Time Limit sett
paragraphs: Ten most prevalent words and word clouds for topics.

be explained by temporal self-appraisal theory, which proposes that individuals often disparage their past selves and compliment their current selves to maintain favorable self-regard (Wilson & Ross, 2001). Past photos refer to users' belief that they do not look good in past photos. An example is the statement "I think a photo looked good when I posted it, but I later realized that it is so ugly." This may be because past photos are less representative of users' current identities, resulting in their decreased willingness to continue sharing them (Ayalon & Toch, 2017). These findings support previous studies which claimed that ephemerality-related designs could support individuals' evolved self-presentations or identities (Huang et al., 2020;Luria & Foulds, 2021). In other words, individuals mitigate inconsistencies in self-presentation between old and new posts by shortening the display duration of post content on social media.

In addition, the topic of past photos reflects that users' worries about their photoshopped photos are an alternative reason for their usage of the Time Limit setting (e.g., "I cannot guarantee that each photo can be photoshopped the same, so I use the three-day limit to avoid others finding flaws.").

Life changes. This topic reflects how users who experience changes in their lives are more likely to use the Time Limit setting. Our results highlight the role of breaking up with romantic partners in Time Limit setting usage. For instance, a typical discussion is as follows: "This is because of my break-up. I am reluctant to delete related posts, but I also don't want others to view them." One possible explanation is past posts are based on original contexts, when individuals experience changes in their lives especially after meaningful events (e.g., marriage or graduation), this past content could not represent their current status and may be misinterpreted in present contexts (Zhao et al., 2013). Also, our finding is consistent with research claiming life changes reduce user willingness to share past content (Ayalon & Toch, 2017). Moreover, life changes may explain why individuals experience evolving self-presentation.

Changes to the social network. This topic reveals that changes to the social network (including size and composition) drive users to use the Time Limit setting, especially when audiences expand as new friends or co-workers (e.g., supervisors and colleagues) are added. For instance, one user chose the Time Limit setting because "I added many people who had to add because of work, but I didn't like to show them my life." Another example is "because I added supervisors, colleagues and some acquaintances." This may be because that it is challenging for users to manage many and multiple audiences over time to achieve impression management, based on the "temporal context collapse" on social media (Huang et al., 2020;Schoenebeck et al., 2016). In particular, users on Moments cannot edit past post content, and they also cannot limit newly added friends' accesses to certain past posts except by blocking them or deleting the old content. As such, they limit the time of content availability to avoid interpersonal uncertainty. This finding is consistent with prior research indicating changes in social network composition could encourage individuals to use the Time Limit setting (Huang et al., 2020;Zhang, Wang, et al., 2021). In addition, changes to the social network may interpret the underlying mechanism for evolving self-presentation.

Burden of impression management. This category reflects how the burden of impression management involved in audience segmentation and old content deletion encourages users to choose the Time Limit setting. Regarding audience segmentation, users often use tags to divide their audience into several groups, but doing so is cumbersome and time-consuming, especially with a large audience (Vitak & Kim, 2014;Zhang, Shi, et al., 2021). Moreover, they must adjust their tags when adding new friends or when certain social relationships evolve, resulting in an increased cognitive burden (Li et al., 2018). A typical statement is "Audience segmentation is tedious." Also, users reported that deleting past posts was time-and energy-consuming (e.g., "Always deleting past posts is so tiring."). As such, to reduce these burdens and achieve impression management, users apply the Time Limit setting to limit the content availability to control accesses of future friends and surveillance from current audiences. Moreover, using the Time Limit setting could avoid others discovering one's audience segmentation behavior (e.g., "I use the three-day limit to avoid embarrassment concerning the exposure of audience segmentation.") as well as satisfy users' needs for reminiscence (e.g., "I feel that my past photos are not good, but I am reluctant to delete them and want to keep them to reminisce, so I make them available for three days.")

These findings support the study of Li et al. (2018) suggesting the burden of audience segment leads individuals to use the Time Limit setting. Additionally, changes in life and social networks may increase the burden of impression management.

Desire to retain mystery. A sense of mystery is a psychological sensation commonly experienced when encountering an unknown or strange phenomenon, and it is often accompanied by strong curiosity (Cooper, 2018). This topic indicates that some users choose the Time Limit setting to retain a sense of mystery. Here is a good example: "Because I want to give others a sense of mystery." Moreover, some users adopt the Time Limit setting to disguise the fact that they rarely post content, which in turn makes them appear mysterious (e.g., "I never post content on Moments, but I can pretend to do so by using the three-day limit, which makes me ). This may be because that keeping mystery could enhance individuals' attractiveness (Baruh & Cemalcılar, 2015), and attractive people often have a clear advantage in their interpersonal relationships (O'Connor & Gladstone, 2018). Thus, users utilize the Time Limit setting to hide their posts automatically and hence generate a sense of mystery. Privacy protection. The topic of privacy protection reflects user concerns about privacy when using social media, and they protect privacy by limiting the display duration of posts. One possible explanation is that, as time passes, users consider certain old content become private, and they do not like their current audiences digging through past posts or new audiences to know too much about them (Zhao et al., 2013), while limiting the content longevity could mitigate these concerns. For instance, one discussion includes, "I don't want new friends to view all my past posts, and this makes me feel I have no privacy at all." Another reason is the use of ephemeral content could avoid certain sensitive information (e.g., ID number, family photos, location information) which is inadvertently disclosed by users be misused by others (e.g., identity theft, fraud) (Bartsch & Dienlin, 2016;Heravi et al., 2018). A typical discussion contains the following: "I use the three-day limit to prevent an offender from defrauding me." Our results are consistent with the study of Zheng and Zhao (2020) which also identified privacy protection drove Time Limit setting use, but they failed to explain this factor in more details. Also, prior research claimed that the use of ephemeral content cloud increase users' perceived control of information and reduce their privacy concerns (Choi et al., 2020;Morlok et al., 2018).

Mood state. This topic indicates that Time Limit setting use is influenced by one's mood state. Mood has been defined as "a host of transient, fluctuating affective states that reflect how an individual feels in general or globally at a particular moment in time" (Berger & Motl, 2000, p. 69). Compared with emotion, mood usually has low intensity and comes without a specific cause or direction (Beedie et al., 2005). It can be divided into two dimensions, positive or negative (Biss et al., 2010). We explain how mood state impacts Time Limit setting use in two ways. First, when users experience a negative mood (e.g., depression), they may want to hide themselves through limiting the visibility of past content (Zheng & Zhao, 2020). An example is: "When I am in a bad mood I use the three-day limit, and I loosen this restriction when I feel better." Moreover, users often share a post when they are in a positive mood (e. g., feeling excited), but they may feel regret later and want to delete it (Sleeper et al., 2013). Hence, users utilize the three-day limit to mitigate their feelings of regret when post content in an excited mood. An example is "I share a post based on my mood at that moment, and later I would feel regret, but deleting it seems deliberate." These findings are aligned with Zheng and Zhao (2020)'s study which claimed emotional factors could impact Time Limit setting use, but they only focused on individuals' bad mood. Also, these findings support those of previous studies showing that mood can impact technology usage and adoption decisions (Karimi & Liu, 2020;Shen, 2015).

Use of substitute platforms. This topic reflects how the use of other social media can impact Time Limit setting usage. Studies have indicated users choose a multiple profile-based strategy to separate selfpresentations via different accounts or platforms to achieve impression management (Vitak, 2015). Some users rarely post content on Moments, but they do disclose information on Weibo as they feel able to be more authentic on this platform. For these users, we explain how the use of substitute platforms influences their Time Limit setting usage in two ways. On the one hand, such users may believe they do not need to use the Time Limit setting, as they post little sensitive information on Moments. An example is: "I do not use the three-day limit, as I do not share emotional content on Moments, but I am willing to share such content on Weibo." On the other hand, users may utilize the three-day limit setting to disguise the fact that they rarely post content, as this feature could make others suppose a user may share a post three days ago. An example statement is: "I hardly disclose information on Moments, so I use the three-day limit setting to pretend to post content three days ago. Actually, I post content on Weibo three times a day." Accordingly, the use of other social media may have a dual influence on Time Limit setting usage.

One possible explanation is that, as time passes, users consider certain old content become private, and they do not like their current audiences digging through past posts or new audiences to know too much about them (Zhao et al., 2013), while limiting the content longevity could mitigate these concerns. For instance, one discussion includes, "I don't want new friends to view all my past posts, and this makes me feel I have no privacy at all." Another reason is the use of ephemeral content could avoid certain sensitive information (e.g., ID number, family photos, location information) which is inadvertently disclosed by users be misused by others (e.g., identity theft, fraud) (Bartsch & Dienlin, 2016;Heravi et al., 2018). A typical discussion contains the following: "I use the three-day limit to prevent an offender from defrauding me." Our results are consistent with the study of Zheng and Zhao (2020) which also identified privacy protection drove Time Limit setting use, but they failed to explain this factor in more details. Also, prior research claimed that the use of ephemeral content cloud increase users' perceived control of information and reduce their privacy concerns (Choi et al., 2020;Morlok et al., 2018).

Mood state. This topic indicates that Time Limit setting use is influenced by one's mood state. Mood has been defined as "a host of transient, fluctuating affective states that reflect how an individual feels in general or globally at a particular moment in time" (Berger & Motl, 2000, p. 69). Compared with emotion, mood usually has low intensity and comes without a specific cause or direction (Beedie et al., 2005). It can be divided into two dimensions, positive or negative (Biss et al., 2010). We explain how mood state impacts Time Limit setting use in two ways. First, when users experience a negative mood (e.g., depression), they may want to hide themselves through limiting the visibility of past content (Zheng & Zhao, 2020). An example is: "When I am in a bad mood I use the three-day limit, and I loosen this restriction when I feel better." Moreover, users often share a post when they are in a positive mood (e. g., feeling excited), but they may feel regret later and want to delete it (Sleeper et al., 2013). Hence, users utilize the three-day limit to mitigate their feelings of regret when post content in an excited mood. An example is "I share a post based on my mood at that moment, and later I would feel regret, but deleting it seems deliberate." These findings are aligned with Zheng and Zhao (2020)'s study which claimed emotional factors could impact Time Limit setting use, but they only focused on individuals' bad mood. Also, these findings support those of previous studies showing that mood can impact technology usage and adoption decisions (Karimi & Liu, 2020;Shen, 2015).

Use of substitute platforms. This topic reflects how the use of other social media can impact Time Limit setting usage. Studies have indicated users choose a multiple profile-based strategy to separate selfpresentations via different accounts or platforms to achieve impression management (Vitak, 2015). Some users rarely post content on Moments, but they do disclose information on Weibo as they feel able to be more authentic on this platform. For these users, we explain how the use of substitute platforms influences their Time Limit setting usage in two ways. On the one hand, such users may believe they do not need to use the Time Limit setting, as they post little sensitive information on Moments. An example is: "I do not use the three-day limit, as I do not share emotional content on Moments, but I am willing to share such content on Weibo." On the other hand, users may utilize the three-day limit setting to disguise the fact that they rarely post content, as this feature could make others suppose a user may share a post three days ago. An example statement is: "I hardly disclose information on Moments, so I use the three-day limit setting to pretend to post content three days ago. Actually, I post content on Weibo three times a day." Accordingly, the use of other social media may have a dual influence on Time Limit setting usage.

sections.paper_id: Zhang et al. - 2022 - “Only visible for three days” Mining microblogs to understand reasons for using the Time Limit sett
Zhang et al. - 2022 - “Only visible for three days” Mining microblogs to understand reasons for using the Time Limit sett
paragraphs: Our work contributes to the literature in the following ways. First, it enriches the literature on ephemerality, which is a relatively new social media affordance, by focusing on reasons for using ephemerality-related settings. Relevant limited research mainly focused on how ephemerality impacted users' attitudes and behaviors (Morlok et al., 2018;Xu et al., 2016) or social interactions (Li et al., 2018). Moreover, we extend ephemerality research by paying attention to social media offering long-term ephemerality (i.e., the Time Limit setting on Moments), while previous studies mainly focused on Snapchat with short-term ephemerality (Luria & Foulds, 2021). However, users' attitudes and behaviors may vary in different ephemerality-related designs (Bainotti et al., 2020). For instance, prior research showed that privacy is not a main motivation for using Snapchat (with short-term ephemerality) (Lemay et al., 2017;Piwek & Joinson, 2016), but our work suggested it is a reason for applying the Time Limit setting (with long-term ephemerality). Thus, our work could extend prior research on the different degree of ephemerality in social media.

Second, we advance understanding of Time Limit setting use by identifying reasons through a text mining approach. Although there were two studies explored this issue through grounded theory (Zhang, Wang, et al., 2021;Zheng & Zhao, 2020), their data was collected by in-depth interview with a small sample size. In particular, Zheng and Zhao (2020) failed to introduce their coding procedure and explain why these factors impacted using Time Limit setting in more details. Similarity, Zhang, Wang, et al. (2021) focused more on proposing a conceptual framework of the Time Limit setting adoption through grounded theory, they did not unpack the influencing factors comprehensively. Moreover, the grounded theory methodology suffers from directionality biases (i.e., consciously of sub-consciously induced in the interpretation) (Debnath et al., 2020). Our work extends and validates findings in these two studies relying on a large volumes of text data and through computer-assisted content analysis in a more efficient and less subjectively biased way (Nelson, 2020).

Finally, our work also extends research on privacy setting use on social media. Although a few studies have discussed the antecedents of using privacy settings on social media, most have focused on general privacy settings and proposed that negative experiences (e.g., privacy concerns and online turbulence), individual characteristics and frequent use of social media are main factors (Litt, 2013;Stern & Salb, 2015). In contrast, our work focuses on a specific popular setting and discusses why users choose this setting by considering its specific features. We find new factors (e.g., the desire to retain a sense of mystery and mood state) relating to privacy setting use on social media, which could inspire future research.

sections.paper_id: Zhang et al. - 2022 - “Only visible for three days” Mining microblogs to understand reasons for using the Time Limit sett
paragraphs: Most current ephemerality-related designs on social media are associated with a "one size fits all" model. Platforms provide users with fixed time frames (e.g., 24 h or three days), and the ephemerality setting is applied for all posts and audiences. Based on our results, we suggest platforms should provide users with more flexible settings to meet their nuanced needs for ephemerality on social media.

First, platforms could allow users to set a specific time span (e.g., one day) for their posts rather than the limited time span options. We find that evolving self-presentation drives Time Limit setting use, but users have different time spans defining how their self-presentation evolves. That is, users have different perceptions of lifespan of their posts. Thus, limited time span options do not conform to actual needs of users. Moreover, time is not the only metric used to divide posts into personal and public regions; the importance of content may be another. For instance, some important and valuable posts (e.g., travel photos) could be displayed longer to represent users' images.

Second, practitioners could enable users to toggle ephemerality settings for individual posts rather than one's entire posting history as well as toggle ephemerality settings for certain audiences rather than one's entire audience. Although our results indicate that Time Limit setting use could mitigate the burden of impression management, doing so would also hinder users from achieving certain impression management goals (e.g., to display meaningful past posts to intended new friends) (Cavalcanti et al., 2017). Therefore, allowing certain posts to be released from ephemerality settings for certain audiences could aid users in impression management.

Third, we find changes in life and social networks are possible factors of Time Limit setting use. As such, platforms could automatically identify relevant changes in users' lives (e.g., graduation or moving to a new place) and social networks (e.g., frequent additions of new friends), and reminder users to hide certain content to avoid interpersonal uncertainty. Moreover, due to the impact of social networks on ephemerality engagement, platforms may seek to design "contact ephemerality" based on interaction activity (Xu et al., 2016), in which platforms could automatically create ephemeral connections instead of permanent contacts in social networks.

Finally, we find some users employ the Time Limit setting to retain a sense of mystery, which may inspire practitioners to improve the norms of ephemerality to help users achieve this goal. For instance, platforms may choose not to show audiences the time span (e.g., three days) users select, as previous studies have indicated that the use of an ephemerality setting may make a negative impression (e.g., as isolated or aloof) on others (Huang et al., 2020;Li et al., 2018).

paragraphs: Our work has several limitations that suggest directions for future research. First, we focused only on the Time Limit setting on Moments, which may limit the generalizability of our results. Future research could examine ephemerality issues with other social media platforms. Moreover, we collected only discussion content regarding the three-day limit, which may limit our understanding of the other two options with lower degrees of ephemerality (i.e., one-month and six-month limits) on Moments. Future studies could discuss user perceptions of different degrees of ephemerality on social media. Second, we crawled only discussion content on Weibo; further studies could collect data from other platforms (e.g., Zhihu). Third, although we extracted eight dimensions through the LDA approach, our coding and validation processes were limited. Due to the limitations of machine learning method (e.g., interpreting arbitrary topics in a defensible way), scholars make a call for human-AI collaboration in qualitative analysis (e.g., the combination of machine learning and grounded theory) (Jiang et al., 2021;Nelson, 2020). Thus, future studies could apply a more efficient mixed method to improve the validity and generalizability of topic results. Finally, our research method could not reveal the causal relationships between relevant topics and Time Limit setting use. Thus, future researchers could propose a research model based our findings and conduct surveys or experiments to verify the relationships between relevant factors and Time Limit setting use. Also, future studies could extend our work by exploring other potential factors (e.g., personal traits) influencing Time Limit setting use as well as how this usage impacts users' attitudes and behaviors in social media.

sections.paper_id: Zhang et al. - 2022 - “Only visible for three days” Mining microblogs to understand reasons for using the Time Limit sett
paragraphs: Understanding factors influencing ephemerality setting use can help practitioners improve ephemeral designs on social media, but few studies have empirically explored this issue. Our work investigates potential reasons for choosing the Time Limit setting, specifically the threeday limit on Moments, through a text mining approach based on discussion content on Weibo. We find that evolving self-presentation, life changes, changes to the social network, the burden of impression management, the desire to retain a sense of mystery, privacy protection, mood state, and the use of substitute platforms are potential influencing factors. We also propose that changes in life and social networks may induce evolving self-presentation and increase the burden of impression management. We hope our findings would encourage scholars to continue exploring ephemerality issues in social media and inspire practitioners to design more effective ephemerality settings to support the evolution of users' online identities. • I think a photo looked good when I posted it, but I later realized that it is so ugly.
